evals.prompt: I'm building a B2B SaaS project management tool targeting small engineering teams (5-50 people). We've done 8 customer interviews and have G2 reviews to draw from. Can you run full customer research — I need personas, JTBD analysis, journey mapping, and segmentation before we finalize our positioning. Write the complete Customer Research Summary to customer-research.md.
evals.expected_output: A complete Customer Research Summary with all four required sections: at least two named personas (each with role, company size, pain points, and buying trigger), at least three JTBD statements in the mandated format, a journey table covering all six stages (Awareness → Advocacy), and a segment prioritization table with at least three segments each showing size, need intensity, accessibility, competition, and a priority rank. Growth direction indicators (INC/DEC/CONST) appear on each segment. No placeholder text anywhere.

deterministic_checks.description: Output contains a Customer Journey section
Output contains a Customer Segments section

deterministic_checks.pattern: When .+, I want to .+, so I can
deterministic_checks.description: At least one JTBD statement uses the mandated 'When ... I want to ... so I can' format
Output contains at least one trend indicator (INC/DEC/CONST)
Customer Journey section references the Awareness stage

expectations: Output contains at least two distinct named personas, each specifying role, company size/industry, at least one pain point, and a buying trigger — not just a generic description.
At least three JTBD statements follow the exact format 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome]' with concrete, non-generic situational context.
The Customer Journey table covers all six stages: Awareness, Consideration, Decision, Purchase, Onboarding, and Retention/Advocacy — with at least one pain point or opportunity identified per stage.
At least three named customer segments appear, each with size (S/M/L), need intensity (H/M/L), accessibility (H/M/L), competition (H/M/L), and a numeric priority rank (1-5).
Each segment row carries an explicit growth direction indicator (INC, DEC, or CONST) and a confidence tier (High/Medium/Low).

evals.prompt: We're entering the HR tech space with an AI-powered performance review tool. What does our ideal customer look like? Give me the persona breakdown — I specifically need their role, company size they work at, their key pain points, what triggers them to look for a new solution, and what information sources they trust. Write the persona to customer-research.md.
evals.expected_output: At least one fully formed persona following the SKILL.md template structure: a persona name and role, company size and industry, stated goals, ranked pain points (major to minor), a representative quote, buying behavior (trigger, research method, decision influence, timeline), and preferred channels. No section left blank or filled with placeholder text.

deterministic_checks.description: Persona contains a Pain Points subsection
Persona contains a Buying Behavior subsection with trigger
Persona includes a representative quote (at least 10 characters in quotes)

expectations: The persona has a concrete name and specific job title (e.g., 'Sarah Chen, VP of People Operations') — not a generic label like 'HR Professional'.
Pain points are ranked from major to minor and are specific to performance review workflows, not generic HR frustrations.
The buying trigger is a specific situational event (e.g., 'company crosses 200 employees', 'failed annual review cycle'), not a vague phrase like 'wants improvement'.
Preferred information channels are named specifically (e.g., LinkedIn groups, SHRM publications, peer referrals) rather than left as broad categories.
Company size is quantified (e.g., '100-500 employees', 'Series B startup') rather than described qualitatively.

evals.prompt: I need to segment our potential customers for a developer-focused API monitoring tool. We're seeing interest from startups, mid-market SaaS companies, and enterprises. Help me build a segmentation matrix I can use to decide which segment to target first, and flag any segments that are growing or shrinking. Write the segmentation matrix and recommendation to customer-research.md.
evals.expected_output: A segmentation table with at least three named segments, each row containing all six required columns (Segment, Size, Need Intensity, Accessibility, Competition, Priority). Each segment must carry a growth direction indicator (INC/DEC/CONST) and a confidence level. A prioritized recommendation for which segment to target first, backed by the matrix data.

deterministic_checks.description: Output contains a markdown table with a Segment column
Segmentation table includes Need Intensity column
At least one segment has a growth direction indicator
Confidence tier appears in the output

expectations: The output includes at least three distinct named segments (e.g., 'Startup (<50 engineers)', 'Mid-Market SaaS (50-500 engineers)', 'Enterprise (500+ engineers)') — not just 'small', 'medium', 'large'.
Every segment row is fully populated — no cell contains H/M/L abbreviations without a legend explaining what they mean, and no column is left blank.
Each segment carries an explicit growth direction (INC, DEC, or CONST) with a one-sentence rationale tying it to a specific market signal.
The priority column uses numeric ranks (1-5) and the recommended first-target segment is named explicitly with reasoning drawn from the matrix data, not stated as a general opinion.
The output flags at least one open question or gap (e.g., 'segment size for enterprise is inferred, not measured') per Mandatory Output Rule #6.

evals.prompt: We just did 12 user interviews with SMB owners considering switching from spreadsheets to accounting software. Map their buying journey from first realizing they have a problem to becoming an advocate, and identify where we should focus to reduce friction. I also need the JTBD statements that capture WHY they're making this switch. Write the journey map and JTBD analysis to customer-research.md.
evals.expected_output: A Customer Journey table covering all six stages (Awareness, Consideration, Decision, Purchase, Onboarding, Retention/Advocacy) with Actions, Thoughts, Emotions, Pain Points, and Opportunities columns populated for each stage. At least three JTBD statements in the exact mandated format covering functional, emotional, and related jobs. Specific opportunities identified at the highest-friction stages.

deterministic_checks.description: Journey table contains an Opportunities column

deterministic_checks.pattern: When .+, I want to .+, so I can
deterministic_checks.description: At least one JTBD statement follows the mandated 'When...I want to...so I can' format
No placeholder values in the output

expectations: The journey table has all six required stage rows (Awareness, Consideration, Decision, Purchase, Onboarding, Retention/Advocacy) with no stage omitted.

Each journey row populates all five columns (Actions, Thoughts, Emotions, Pain Points, Opportunities) with content specific to SMB owners switching from spreadsheets — not generic software-purchase journey text.

At least three JTBD statements appear, covering at minimum: one functional job (core task with measurable outcome), one emotional job (how they want to feel), and one related job (before/after the core job).
Every JTBD statement uses the exact format 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome]' — not paraphrased into bullet points or narrative prose.

The output names at least two specific high-friction stages and proposes a concrete opportunity at each (e.g., 'During Onboarding, SMBs struggle with data import — opportunity: one-click spreadsheet import wizard'), not a generic 'improve UX' recommendation.
